one_line_takeaway: VocBulwark freezes the vocoder and injects a lightweight, acoustically-entangled 'Temporal Adapter' watermark plus a coarse-to-fine gated extractor, achieving near-clean-baseline fidelity (e.g. DiffWave PESQ 3.34 vs clean 3.51) at 100 bps while beating prior generative watermarkers Groot/RIWF/HiFi-GANw on Codec and compound-attack extraction accuracy (e.g. 99.00% vs Groot's 76.23% under Opus+MP3) using 99.3% fewer trainable parameters than Groot.
key_finding: On the in-distribution LJSpeech fidelity table (Table 1), VocBulwark on DiffWave (Ours[DW], 100 bps) attains STOI 0.9605 / PESQ 3.3398 / SSIM 0.8519 / DNSMOS 3.2727 / ACC 0.9998, closely tracking the unwatermarked DiffWave backbone (STOI 0.9655, PESQ 3.5113) while embedding at a much higher bit-rate (100 bps) than most post-hoc baselines (16-32 bps); on BigVGAN, Ours[BVG] reaches PESQ 3.4941 versus the clean BigVGAN's 3.3397 (i.e., PESQ is not degraded relative to the unwatermarked backbone in this case). Under common attacks (Table 2), Ours[DW] maintains 99.86% ACC under 5 dB Gaussian noise versus Groot's 99.13% and RIWF's 76.90% (a >22-point margin over RIWF); under variable-length attacks, Ours[DW] achieves 99.94% ACC under 0.9x time-stretch and 99.31% under cropping, whereas WavMark and Groot report no results ("-") for those columns, i.e., are not evaluated there. Under Codec attacks (Table 3, per text on p.7-8), VocBulwark's average Opus-compression accuracy is 99.69% versus RIWF's 61.91% and HiFi-GANw's 90.40%, edging out Groot's 99.40%; against non-EnCodec neural codecs (HiFi-Codec, TiCodec, STCodec, FACodec) it holds 95.14% average ACC versus HiFi-GANw's 57.18%, RIWF's 70.35%, and Groot's 78.50%. Under the most severe compound attacks (Table 4), VocBulwark achieves 99.00% ACC on Opus+MP3 and 96.48% on Opus+EnCodec, versus Groot's 76.23% and 75.20% respectively -- roughly a 20-point gap the authors attribute to the acoustic-space coupling of the TA. On efficiency (Table 5), VocBulwark uses 1.66-1.76M trainable parameters and 40-60 MB model size versus Groot's 250.28M parameters and 984.99 MB, a reported 99.3% parameter reduction and 24x storage compression, with comparable or better inference latency (e.g., Ours[HFG] 13.48+/-2.81 ms vs HiFi-GANw's 15.47+/-8.14 ms). Capacity scaling on LJSpeech shows the DiffWave variant sustains >99% extraction accuracy up to 2000 bps with only a modest SSIM decrease (~0.85 to ~0.80), leading the authors to identify 2000 bps as a practical upper capacity limit.
core_contribution: VocBulwark is a generative speech watermarking framework that injects a watermark into a vocoder's generation process via an 'additional-parameter injection' strategy rather than by editing the vocoder's native weights or fine-tuning it. It freezes the pretrained generative backbone (DiffWave, PriorGrad, HiFi-GAN, or BigVGAN) and adds a lightweight, trainable 'Temporal Adapter' (TA) that fuses a binary watermark into the model's intermediate acoustic (hidden) features via Acoustic Feature Alignment, Frame-level Temporal Broadcasting (to make the embedding time-invariant/desync-resistant), and an Adaptive Injection block (concat + downsample + depth-wise separable conv + zero-conv + residual add). Recovery is performed by a paired 'Coarse-to-Fine Gated Extractor' (Cage), built from Gated Separable Convolution Modules (GSCM) with coarse/mid/fine-grained multi-scale feature aggregation and dual-path (average+max) pooling, trained against an Attack Simulator of eight mild distortions. A third contribution, the 'Accuracy-Guided Optimization Curriculum,' is a threshold-gated (tau1=0.9, tau2=0.95) loss-weighting schedule that prioritizes watermark-extraction accuracy early in training and shifts weight toward fidelity/perceptual loss once extraction accuracy stabilizes, to resolve the fidelity-vs-robustness optimization conflict inherent to jointly training embedding and extraction objectives.
threat_model: The paper defines an explicit three-party threat model (Section 3): (1) Users query a TTS pipeline (acoustic model M + vocoder G_psi) for speech s=TTS(m); (2) the Model Provider embeds a per-model watermark identifier w into generated speech (s_hat=G_psi(c,w)) to later extract w_hat via an extraction function f_D, in order to establish model provenance/IP protection and support content-regulation mandates the paper cites (EU AI Act 2024, US S.4178, China GB 45438-2025); (3) the Adversary operates in a strict black-box setting with no access to model parameters, architecture, or the watermarking algorithm, and can only manipulate the disseminated waveform s_hat. Four adversary attack classes are defined: Common Attacks (Gaussian/pink noise, low/band/high-pass filtering, echo, dither), Variable-length Attacks (time-stretching, cropping, which desynchronize post-hoc watermarks), Codec Attacks (compression/regeneration via traditional codecs MP3/Opus and neural codecs EnCodec/HiFi-Codec/TiCodec/STCodec/FACodec), and Compound Attacks (sequential combinations, e.g. Opus+MP3, Opus+EnCodec). The paper's own framing of the security goal is model-provenance/IP protection and regulatory traceability for AI-generated speech, not detection of deepfakes per se and not a cryptographic unforgeability guarantee -- extraction accuracy (bit accuracy, ACC) is the operative security metric.
limitations: The paper does not include a dedicated 'Limitations' section (only an Impact Statement flagging dual-use risk: the watermarking technology 'holds the potential for misuse, such as unauthorized surveillance or copyright infringement'). Methodologically notable gaps evident from the paper: (1) baselines WavMark and Groot are reported with missing ('-') entries for the time-stretch/cropping columns in Table 2, so the variable-length robustness comparison against those two specific baselines is incomplete rather than a like-for-like win; (2) the Attack Simulator trains the extractor on only eight mild distortions, and the authors themselves frame Codec/compound-attack robustness as an extrapolation/generalization claim beyond that training distribution rather than an attack class the model was directly optimized against; (3) the threat model is explicitly restricted to a black-box adversary with no access to model weights, architecture, or the watermarking algorithm -- the paper does not evaluate a white-box or adaptive adversary who knows the TA/Cage design and could attempt targeted removal or forgery; (4) evaluation is limited to four specific vocoder backbones and three speech datasets (one of which, AiShell3, is the sole cross-lingual/Mandarin test), so generalization to other languages, more vocoder architectures, or full end-to-end TTS/LALM pipelines beyond the vocoder stage is not directly demonstrated in the extracted sections.

quantitative_results: LJSpeech fidelity, Ours[DW] (100 bps): STOI 0.9605, PESQ 3.3398, SSIM 0.8519, MCD 6.3682, DNSMOS 3.2727, ACC 0.9998 (vs clean DiffWave STOI 0.9655, PESQ 3.5113)
LJSpeech fidelity, Ours[BVG] (100 bps): STOI 0.9795, PESQ 3.4941, ACC 1.0000 (vs clean BigVGAN STOI 0.9777, PESQ 3.3397)
5 dB Gaussian noise ACC: Ours[DW] 99.86% vs Groot 99.13% vs RIWF 76.90%
Variable-length attack ACC: Ours[DW] 99.94% under 0.9x time-stretch, 99.31% under cropping
Opus compression average ACC: VocBulwark 99.69% vs Groot 99.40% vs HiFi-GANw 90.40% vs RIWF 61.91%
Non-EnCodec neural codec average ACC: VocBulwark 95.14% vs Groot 78.50% vs RIWF 70.35% vs HiFi-GANw 57.18%
Compound attack Opus+MP3 ACC: VocBulwark 99.00% vs Groot 76.23%
Compound attack Opus+EnCodec ACC: VocBulwark 96.48% vs Groot 75.20%
Trainable parameters/model size: VocBulwark (DiffWave variant) 1.66M / 40.06MB vs Groot 250.28M / 984.99MB (reported 99.3% parameter reduction, 24x storage compression)
Inference time: Ours[HFG] 13.48±2.81 ms vs HiFi-GANw 15.47±8.14 ms
Capacity scaling: DiffWave variant sustains >99% extraction accuracy up to 2000 bps with SSIM decreasing from ~0.85 to ~0.80
AiShell3 (OOD) fidelity: Ours[DW] PESQ 2.5057, STOI 0.9097 vs clean DiffWave PESQ 2.6435; Ours[BVG] PESQ 3.4325 vs clean BigVGAN PESQ 3.4614
